questions.question: What makes a review checklist more useful than general feedback like 'go deeper'?
options: It ties feedback to specific answer stages and missing evidence rather than vague impressions.
It removes the need for any follow-up questions.
It guarantees the same score across all reviewers automatically.
It focuses only on architectural style.
questions.explanation: Structured reviews reveal which dimensions were missing and why that mattered.

questions.question: Which section is most often forgotten in weaker system design answers?
options: Failure modes, observability, and trade-offs near the end of the answer.
The title of the prompt.
The name of the data center region.
A list of programming languages.
questions.explanation: Weak answers often stop at the diagram and never close the loop operationally.

questions.question: Why should a review checklist ask for evidence instead of only a score?
options: Evidence makes the feedback actionable and lowers the chance of reviewer bias or vague praise.
Scores are never useful in any context.
Evidence guarantees that all technical choices were correct.
It allows the reviewer to skip naming next actions.
questions.explanation: The learner needs to see what in the answer justified the review conclusion.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with using one short checklist across many prompts?
options: It improves consistency and comparison, but may miss some prompt-specific nuance unless supplemented verbally.
It increases review cost but lowers consistency.
It makes deep dives unnecessary.
It prevents company-specific mocks entirely.
questions.explanation: Reusable checklists are powerful, but they cannot encode every special case.

questions.question: If an answer is technically correct but never reaches failure modes, how should the review treat it?
options: As fully strong because the main architecture was right.
As incomplete, because senior-level signal includes reliability and operational reasoning.
As invalid only if the prompt was about security.
As impossible to evaluate without writing code.
questions.explanation: Operational completeness is part of system design quality, not an optional add-on.

questions.question: Which observability signal would help improve the checklist itself?
options: Which checklist categories recur most often as weak across many reviews.
How many markdown headers are in the learner's notes.
The number of services in each architecture diagram.
The cloud provider selected for each prompt.
questions.explanation: Recurring weak categories reveal where the checklist and training emphasis may need tuning.

questions.question: What is the most valuable review summary to give a learner after a mock?
options: One strength, one critical gap, and one next drill backed by evidence from the answer.
A long list of every possible improvement with no prioritization.
A numeric score only.
A rewritten perfect answer to memorize.
questions.explanation: Priority and evidence matter more than exhaustive commentary.

questions.question: How should the checklist adapt for a 20-minute drill?
options: It should keep the same core dimensions but compress the expected depth per category.
It should drop sizing entirely to save time.
It should focus only on diagrams and ignore wrap-up quality.
It should replace evidence notes with intuition.
questions.explanation: Short drills still need the same reasoning categories, just with scaled expectations.
